s: The 'Wilanów of the North' — with a bison menagerie
w: Saxon minister Heinrich von Brühl had this built 1752-58; kings Augustus III and Stanisław August Poniatowski came to visit the menagerie of bison and exotic animals. The locals called it the 'Wilanów of the North.' Bought in 1876 by the Wawelberg and Rotwand banking families who kept Bielany's grandest ruin in private hands.
_facts: Built 1752-1758 by Johann Friedrich Knöbel for Heinrich von Brühl, Saxon minister of state
Augustus III and Stanisław August Poniatowski both visited; estate had a menagerie including bison
Called 'Wilanów of the North' by contemporaries
Purchased 1876 by the Wawelberg and Rotwand banking families
Now a ruinous shell within Młociny park, publicly accessible

s: Armoured trains in the old main terminus
w: Opened 2016 in the old Warsaw Główna terminus, this is where the armoured trains live — one of only a handful surviving in all of Europe. The first Railway Museum was gutted in war and scattered; the new curators rebuilt from scratch inside walls that once moved an empire's freight. Iron and smoke memory.
_facts: Founded 15 June 2015 as a state railway museum institution; opened to public Night of Museums, 14 May 2016
Located at Towarowa 3, in the former Warsaw Główna passenger terminus building
Holds one of the few surviving armoured trains in Europe
Successor institution to the Muzeum Kolejnictwa which closed 2011
Permanent collection includes steam locomotives, rolling stock, uniforms, timetables

s: Tsarist earthwork ring — moat and drawbridge survive
w: One ring of the tsarist Warsaw Fortress, earthworks from the 1880s raised to hem in a subject city the tsar never quite trusted. Fort IIA still has its moat and remnants of a drawbridge; the Military University of Technology campus grew up around it. The empire fell, the fort stayed.
_facts: Fort IIA (Radiowo) is part of the outer ring of the 19th-century Warsaw Fortress built by Russian Empire
Dating to the 1880s; supplementary defensive work guarding western approaches
Moat and earthworks substantially intact; traces of original drawbridge structure remain
Military University of Technology (WAT) campus now surrounds the site
Part of a ring of forts most of which have been demolished or built over

s: Royal hunting ground, baby's grave, presidential retreat
w: Sobieski hunted here in the 17th century; by 1782 Szymon Zug had raised a classicist palace for the Czartoryski family. Renamed Natolin in 1807 after a baby who died here, it acquired a Roman aqueduct and Moorish gate under Henryk Marconi; Bierut used it as a presidential retreat. Now a College of Europe campus.
_facts: 17th-century royal hunting ground of Jan III Sobieski
Classicist palace built 1780-82 by Szymon Bogumił Zug for Izabela and Adam Kazimierz Czartoryski
Renamed Natolin 1807 after the infant Natalia Potocka who died on the estate
Redesigned 1834-38 by Henryk Marconi who added a Doric temple, Roman-style aqueduct and Moorish gate
Served as presidential residence for Bolesław Bierut after WWII; since 1993 is Warsaw campus of College of Europe

s: Potocki family plot that became a national one
w: Potocki family cemetery founded 1816 around Piotr Aigner's neo-Gothic mausoleum chapel, expanded to a Greek cross plan by 1860 when Henryk Marconi added the ceramic fence. Among the graves: fighters of the 1863 uprising, soldiers of September 1939, and 171 from the Warsaw Uprising battalions Baszta and Waligóra. A family plot that became a national one.
_facts: Founded 1816 by the Potocki family on their Wilanów estate
Neo-Gothic Potocki mausoleum-chapel designed by Piotr Aigner, built 1823-24, at centre of original circular plan
Expanded to Greek-cross plan around 1860; ceramic fence by Henryk Marconi added at same time
Contains graves of 1863 January Uprising fighters, September 1939 campaign soldiers
171 fighters from Warsaw Uprising battalions Baszta and Waligóra are buried here

s: 107 shot in one night — December 1939's first mass reprisal
w: On the night of 26-27 December 1939, German troops shot 107 Polish civilians in Wawer — one of the first mass reprisal executions of the occupation. Two German soldiers had been killed by local criminals; the response was to arrest 120 men aged 16 to 70 at random, try them in a show trial, and shoot 107. The street is now named '27 Grudnia.'
_facts: Night of 26-27 December 1939: 107 Polish civilians executed in Wawer by German forces
Reprisal for the killing of two German soldiers by Stanisław Dąbek and Marian Domagała (criminals, not resistance fighters)
120 men arrested at random from ages 16 to 70; show trial held; 114 sentenced to death, 107 executed, 7 survived
One of the first large-scale mass executions in German-occupied Poland
The local street is named ulica 27 Grudnia in commemoration; memorial plaque at execution site

w: Founded 1409-11 by Duchess Danuta Anna as the first parish church for Nowa Warszawa, the city that grew up across the creek from the medieval Old Town. Gothic brick, three naves, older than any current building in the area by centuries. Warsaw's second city began here, on a hill above the Vistula.
_facts: Founded 1409-1411 by Duchess Danuta Anna of Jedrzejów (wife of Janusz I the Elder)
Served as original parish church for Nowa Warszawa (New Warsaw), the settlement north of Old Town
Gothic brick construction, three naves; at ul. Przyrynek 2
One of the oldest surviving church buildings in Warsaw
Predates the current buildings in New Town by several centuries

s: Dystopian painter's building — where he was murdered
w: Painter Beksiński lived and worked at Sonaty 6 in this composer-named estate — and was stabbed to death there in February 2005 by a 19-year-old he refused to lend money. The 2007 mural by Piotr Janowczyk covers the wall of the building where it happened. His dystopian canvases are world famous; the building is unremarkable. That's the point.
_facts: Zdzisław Beksiński (1929-2005) lived at ul. Sonaty 6 in the Służew nad Dolinką estate from 1977
Murdered 21 February 2005 by 19-year-old Robert Kupiec, who stabbed him 17 times after being refused a loan of 100 PLN
Mural by Piotr Janowczyk painted in 2007 on the facade of Sonaty 6
The estate has streets named after composers: Bach, Mozart, Elsner, Noskowski, Batuty, Sonaty
Beksiński's paintings are in major collections worldwide; he was also a photographer and sculptor

s: Composer-named concrete — Warsaw's most storied estate
w: Designed 1973-78 by Janusz Nowak, Piotr Sembrat and Jerzy Kuźmienka, the H-frame panels come in four colours to navigate by. Streets are named after composers — Bach, Mozart, Elsner, Noskowski. Beksiński was murdered here; rapper Tede grew up here; Barej shot Zmiennicy here. Warsaw's most storied concrete patch.
_facts: Built 1973-1978; architects Janusz Nowak, Piotr Sembrat, Jerzy Kuźmienka
H-frame prefab panel technology; colour-coded sectors (red, blue, green, black panels)
Streets named after composers: Batuty, Sonaty, Koncertowa, Wieniawskiego, Bacewicz
Beksiński murdered at Sonaty 6 in 2005; rapper Tede grew up on the estate
Stanisław Bareja filmed parts of Zmiennicy here; estate has 3,000 trees

s: The only Witkacy footage — and the only Grażyna manuscript
w: Spread across six Old Town townhouses including two from the 15th century, this holds the only surviving manuscript of Mickiewicz's Grażyna and the only known film footage of Witkacy — digitally restored in 2015 after decades in an archive. The national literary imagination compressed into three floors of medieval stone.
_facts: Founded 1950; at Rynek Starego Miasta 20, occupying six townhouses including 15th-century Balcerowska and Orlemusowska
Holds the only surviving autograph manuscript of Mickiewicz's Grażyna
Has the only known film footage of Stanisław Ignacy Witkiewicz (Witkacy), digitally restored 2015
Collection includes manuscripts, portraits, personal effects of major Polish writers
Part of the reconstructed Old Town Market Square complex (rebuilt after WWII destruction)
